flashcards.back: Search is a fundamental problem-solving technique in AI that involves systematically exploring a space of possible states to find a path from an initial state to a goal state.
The complete set of all possible configurations that the problem solver may encounter while searching for a solution.
A tree structure where each node represents a state, and edges represent transitions between states. The root is the initial state.
Search strategies that use problem-specific knowledge beyond the definition of the problem itself to find solutions more efficiently.
A function that estimates the cost from the current state to the goal state, guiding the search towards promising paths.
BFS is complete - if a solution exists, BFS will find it given infinite resources.
DFS may get stuck in infinite loops if the state space contains cycles and no mechanism to detect visited states.
An optimal search finds the best solution (minimum cost) among all possible solutions.
The average number of children per node in the search tree, a key factor affecting search complexity.
An informed search that expands the node closest to the goal, using only the heuristic function h(n) for evaluation.
A search that combines actual cost g(n) and heuristic estimate h(n): f(n) = g(n) + h(n), guaranteeing optimal solution if h is admissible.
A heuristic that never overestimates the cost to reach the goal, i.e., h(n) ≤ h*(n) where h* is the true cost.
A recursive algorithm for two-player zero-sum games that assumes optimal play, minimizing the maximum loss.
An optimization of Minimax that eliminates branches that cannot influence the final decision, reducing search complexity.
A greedy local search algorithm that continuously moves in the direction of increasing value to find the peak.
